coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive immunoassay of glycoprotein 125 (CA 125) in untreated human plasma samples using poly (CTAB‑chitosan) doped with silver nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Mohammadzadeh, Arezoo
Jafari, Mohsen
Habibi, Biuck
coredata.dc:description: CA 125 is a Mucin glycoprotein and its concentration in human serum correlates with a woman's risk of developing ovarian cancer and also indicates response to therapy in diagnosed patients. Accurate detection of this large, complex protein in patient biofluids is of great clinical relevance. In this work, an innovative immunoassay for quantitation of CA 125 based on signal amplification strategy was proposed. In this work, Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) labeled CA 125-antibody (anti-CA 125) was immobilized onto a green and biocompatible nanocomposite containing poly cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (P (CTAB) as conductive matrix, chitosan (CS) as biocompatible agent and sliver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) as signal amplification element. Therefore, a novel nanocomposite film based P (CTAB-CS) and Ag NPs was exploited to develop a highly sensitive immunosensor for detection of CA 125 protein. Importantly, Ag NPs prepared by electrodeposition method which lead to compact morphology. Fully electrochemical methodology was used to prepare a new transducer on a glassy carbon surface which provided a high surface area to immobilize a high amount of the anti-CA 125. The surface morphology of electrode was characterized by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The immunosensor was employed for the detection of CA 125 using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPVs) techniques. Under optimized condition the calibration curve for CA 125 concentration by SWV and DPV was linear in 0.01–400 U/mL with lower limit of quantification of 0.001 U/mL. The method was successfully applied assay of the CA 125 in unprocessed human plasma samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ovarian cancer
Immunoassay
Carcinoma antigen 125
Immunocomplex
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrodeposition of Ag NPs in the presence of CTAB
Electrodeposition of Ag NPs in the presence of CS
Electrodeposition of Ag NPs in the presence of CTAB and CS
Immobilization of monoclonal antibody on the surface of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs and preparation of immunosensor
Analytical characteristic of the CA 125 immunosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) CVs for electrodeposition of Ag NPs on the surface of GCE in the presence of organic template (CTAB 0.18 g) in the 1 mM of AgNO3 solved in 0.1 M HNO3. A) CVs for electrodeposition of Ag NPs on the surface of GCE in the presence of biocompatible agent (CS 0.0025 g) in the 1 mM of AgNO3 solved in 0.1 M HNO3. CVs for electrodeposition of Ag NPs on the surface of GCE in the presence of organic template (CTAB 0.18 g) and biocompatible agent (CS) in the 1 mM of AgNO3 solved in 0.1 M HNO3. No cycle = 20, sweep rate is 50 mV/s, potential rage is; −1.2 to 1.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl.
A–G) FE-SEM images of bare GCE, P (CTAB)-Ag NPs-GCE, P (CS)-Ag NPs-GCE, P (CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE, P (CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-Ab-GCE, P (CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-Ab-BSA-CA 125-GCE, respectively.
(A–E) EDS of P (CTAB)-Ag NPs-GCE, P (CS)-Ag NPs-GCE, P (CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE, P (CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-Ab-GCE, P (CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-Ab-BSA-CA 125-GCE, respectively.

CVs of GCE modified by P(CTAB)-Ag NPs, P(CS)-Ag NPs, P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs, P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE after incubation with HRP labeled monoclonal antibody (anti-CA 125), P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125 after blocking with BSA. P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA after incubation with CA 125 antigen. Scan Rate = 0.05 V/s. Supporting media is 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− containing KCl. Inset; DPVs of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/Ab before and after incubation with CA 125 antigen.
A–C) CVs of P(CTAB)-Ag NPs, P(CS)-Ag NPs, P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs, P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE in 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− containing KCl, respectively. D) Dependency of peak current versus sweep rates.

A, B) DPVs of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA in different concentration of CA 125 (A: 15, 50, 100, 200, and 400 U/mL and B: 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, and 0.5 U/mL) in the solution 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− containing KCl. Step potential = 0.001, amplitude time = 0.0 2 s, pulse amplitude = 10 Hz, scan rate = 0.05 V/s. Insets are calibration curve.

A) SWVs of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA in different concentration of CA 125 (A: 15, 50, 100, 200, and 400 U/mL and B: 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, and 0.5 U/mL) in the solution 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− containing KCl. Step potential = 0.001, amplitude time = 0.0 2 s, frequency = 10 Hz, scan rate = 0.05 V/s. Insets are calibration curve.

A, B) DPVs of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA in different concentration of CA 125 (A: 15, 50, 100, 200, and 400 U/mL and B: 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, and 0.5 U/mL) in human plasma samples in the solution 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− containing KCl. Step potential = 0.001, amplitude time = 0.0 2 s, pulse amplitude = 10 Hz, scan rate = 0.05 V/s. C, D) calibration curve.

A) SWVs of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA in different concentration of CA 125 (A: 15, 50, 100, 200, and 400 U/mL and B: 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, and 0.5 U/mL) in human plasma samples in the solution 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− containing KCl. Step potential = 0.001, amplitude time = 0.0 2 s, frequency = 10 Hz, scan rate = 0.05 V/s. C, D) calibration curve.
A) DPVs of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA/CA 125 in different time of incubation. B) CVs of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE in different cycle No.

A, B) DPVs and SWVs P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA in the presence of different interferent species (BSA, CA 15-3, PSA, glucose, and uric acid) in the solution 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− containing KCl, respectively. Parameters: potential range of −0.15 V to 0.8 V with a step potential of 5 mV, step potential = 0.001, amplitude time = 0.02 s, frequency = 10 Hz, pulse amplitude = 0.05 scan rate = 0.05 V/s. CA 125 concentration is 15 U/mL. C, D) The related histograms.
A comparison of the performance of several immunosensor for the detection of CA 125.

row.entry: Surface plasmon resonance and capacitive immunosensors label-free

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery analysis of the CA-125 specific immunosensor in healthy human plasma samples.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive immunoassay of glycoprotein 125 (CA 125) in untreated human plasma samples using poly (CTAB‑chitosan) doped with silver nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz 53714-161, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CA 125 is a Mucin glycoprotein and its concentration in human serum correlates with a woman's risk of developing ovarian cancer and also indicates response to therapy in diagnosed patients. Accurate detection of this large, complex protein in patient biofluids is of great clinical relevance. In this work, an innovative immunoassay for quantitation of CA 125 based on signal amplification strategy was proposed. In this work, Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) labeled CA 125-antibody (anti-CA 125) was immobilized onto a green and biocompatible nanocomposite containing poly cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (P (CTAB) as conductive matrix, chitosan (CS) as biocompatible agent and sliver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) as signal amplification element. Therefore, a novel nanocomposite film based P (CTAB-CS) and Ag NPs was exploited to develop a highly sensitive immunosensor for detection of CA 125 protein. Importantly, Ag NPs prepared by electrodeposition method which lead to compact morphology. Fully electrochemical methodology was used to prepare a new transducer on a glassy carbon surface which provided a high surface area to immobilize a high amount of the anti-CA 125. The surface morphology of electrode was characterized by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The immunosensor was employed for the detection of CA 125 using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPVs) techniques. Under optimized condition the calibration curve for CA 125 concentration by SWV and DPV was linear in 0.01–400 U/mL with lower limit of quantification of 0.001 U/mL. The method was successfully applied assay of the CA 125 in unprocessed human plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, the second leading cause of death, is a group of diseases involving abnormal cell growth with the potential to invade other parts of the body. The survival of cancer patients is strongly associated with the stage of the tumor at the time of diagnosis. Therefore, the implementation of highly sensitive and specific progression monitoring for timely cancer diagnosis is of great clinical connotation [1]. To this end, molecular recognition probes such as monoclonal antibodies have significantly improved the performance of routine cancer diagnostics. The life-time risk of having ovarian cancer is 1 in 70 women's. CA 125 that counting as the seventh cause of cancer-related death among women is a repeating peptide epitope of the Mucin 16, which promotes cancer cell proliferation, and currently is the best serum-based tumor marker for ovarian cancer [2]. CA-125 is the most frequently used biomarker for ovarian cancer detection. Medical societies including American Congress of Obstetricians and Gynecologists recommend against women with average risk of ovarian cancer having routine CA-125 screening or other screening for this cancer. Reasons for this include evidence that ambiguous test results are more likely to lead to further invasive, harmful, and unnecessary health care than they are likely to detect ovarian cancer in women who are at average risk of developing it [2]. Normal values range from 0 to 35 U/mL. Elevated levels in post-menopausal women are usually an indication that further screening is necessary. In pre-menopausal women, the test is less reliable as values are often elevated due to a number of non-cancerous causes, and a value above 35 is not necessarily a cause for concern.

Therefore, the quantitative detection of CA 125 in serum will be valuable for early diagnosis because ovarian cancer is usually asymptomatic until advanced stages. In recent years, a variety of immunological and biochemical methods, such as radioimmunoassay, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [3], electrochemiluminescent immunoassay [4] chemiluminescence [5], electrochemical [6], piezoelectric immunosensors [7], and field effect transistors [8] have been developed for CA 125 detection. Even though they have the advantages of being selective and sensitive, these techniques have the disadvantages of a high cost, long analysis time, the need for qualified personnel and sophisticated instrumentation; also their sensitivity is not high enough to detect tumors at an early stage.

Due to the extremely low concentration of tumor markers in blood, one of the key issues in detecting tumor markers is the sensitivity of the developed methods [9–11]. In order to achieve the lower detection limit and higher sensitivity, signal amplification techniques have been employed for amplifying response signal to detect low level of biomarkers for developing ultrasensitive immunoassay [12,13].

In recent years, given the advancement of technology, the way for promotion of the rapid and sensitive diagnosis methods have been paved, and the dreams of disease prevention before treatment comes true. Regarding the public health issues, highly sensitive sensor systems are progressing for early stage detection of disease to reduce the risk of mortality [14,15]. Hence, the strong demand for employing cheap, efficient, fast, simple, portable, and cost effective methods for immunoassay have been boosted. Among various detection tools, electrochemical methods, not only cover all these highlighted features, but also provide highly sensitive and selective biosensing strategies [16]. In order to achieve the lower detection limit and higher sensitivity, signal amplification techniques have been employed for amplifying response signal to detect low level of biomarkers for developing ultrasensitive electrochemical immunoassay.

Recently, signal amplification has been successfully performed by advanced nanomaterials. Nanoscale materials offer some outstanding prospects for interfacing biological recognition events with transduction of electronic signals and for designing new bioelectronic devices exhibiting with novel functions [16,17]. Since the discovery of the conducting polymers in 1977s, many scientists have been working on finding the applications for the newly introduced polymers and creating a pathway in the sensing world [18]. Thanks to their unique characters, including biocompatibility, fascinating electrocatalytic activity, large surface area, excellent conductivity, and stability, which is coupled with their ease of processing/modification and rich chemistry; polymer based electrochemical sensors were noticed as a promising platform for the sensitive detection of biological compounds [19–22]. The goal of the covalent attachment of biomolecules to the surface of polymer could be achieved by using polymer derivatives (e.g. carboxylated or aminated), which provides plentiful of functional groups for long term retention and stabilization of biomolecules [23–26]. Considering the electrochemical methods, electrochemical polymerization not only provide a simple and reproducible formation of polymer, but also permits easy control over the properties of the polymeric coating such as morphology and thickness, compare to other robust methods (e.g. chemicals) [27,28].

Surfactants are a kind of amphiphilic ion or molecule. They usually have a hydrophilic head, which is compatible with water and a long hydrophobic tail which is compatible with oil [29]. The application of various surfactants in electrochemistry has been reported in many papers [30–32]. The surfactant may change the electrochemical process by adsorbing or aggregating at the electrode interface [33]. The cationic surfactant-CTAB (cetyl trimethylammonium bromide) has been employed for the electroanalysis of some biomolecules such as naphthol, ciprofloxacin, atorvastatin and cis‑jasmone [34–37]. The presence of CTAB in the electrolyte solution remarkably enhanced the electrochemical responses of the analytes.

Only a few studies dealt with the electrochemical behavior or determination of CTAB molecule [38,39]. Recently, electropolymerization of CTAB in acidic medium and application of this polymer modified surface for the simultaneous determination of some biomolecules (dopamine, uric acid, tryptophan and theophylline) has been first time reported by chines researches [40].

A review of the literature revealed that, the intensity of peak current obtained by poly (CTAB) is low. Also, stability of this polymer on the surface of electrode is very small. Therefore, we need to use some of metals such as gold, iron, silver, and palladium to enhance the peak currents which is necessary for determination trace amount of analytes in real samples. So, in the present study, electrochemically synthesized silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are used for enhancing the signal for electrochemical application. On the other hand we need to use a biocompatible and stable compound for the increasing of durability of CTAB. Therefore, chitosan was selected as the best candidate for this purpose. Biocompatible polymer chitosan (CS) produced by treating shrimp and other crustacean shells with the alkali sodium hydroxide has been extensively used in sensor technology. Due to its attractive properties, that includes excellent film-forming ability, high permeability, good adhesion, nontoxicity, cheapness and a susceptibility to chemical modification. It also facilitates the electron transfer after its swelling in the reaction mixture due to its hydrophilic nature [40–42]. Besides, chitosan can be used as a dispersant to form stable composite which can form a stable film on the electrode surface [43]. CS as a polysaccharide biopolymer, which displays excellent film-forming ability, high water permeability, good adhesion, and susceptibility to chemical modifications due to the presence of reactive amino and hydroxyl functional groups [44,45].

Therefore, P(CTAB-CS) was used as an organic matric for stabilization of AgNPs on the surface of GCE which was prepared by electrodeposition method. So, the final nanocomposite was P(CTAB-CS)-AgNPs. While the electrochemical synthesis of AgNPs is well established [46,47], the scheme presented herein enables particularly short deposition times, minimal solution preparation, and no requirement of an external linker or functionalizing ligand. Moreover, in-situ tailoring of the nanoparticle surface chemistry resulted in an improved affinity and selectivity [48].

In summary, these improved performances encouraged us to explore the possible leading role played by the presence of AgNPs on the structure of P(CTAB-CS). The proposed nanocomposite P(CTAB-CS)-AgNPs increases the contact area with the analyte (CA 125 biomarker), so they could increase the electrochemical active surface to interact with CA 125. Since the increase in geometric surface area is very important parameter in electrochemistry, therefore modification of P(CTAB-CS) with AgNPs can increase the rate of electrochemical reaction. Therefore, integration of AgNPs into P(CTAB-CS) can be providing an electrically conductive film towards increases Faradic currents. Excellent properties of P(CTAB-CS) and the advantages of AgNPs have received more attention for preparation of high performance electrode material. It was for these reasons that we decided to explore the possibility of using the P(CTAB-CS)-AgNPs as a platform for the development of electrochemical sensor for detection of CA 125. Finally, we investigated the fully electrochemical preparation of P(CTAB-CS)-AgNPs on the surface of GCE as a novel strategy for interface science. The formation of P(CTAB-CS)-AgNPs was firstly monitored. The latent activity of P(CTAB-CS)-AgNPs coating towards detection and determination of CA 125 was then investigated. The redox behavior of prepared immunosensor was then characterized by CV and DPV. Then, we used P(CTAB-CS)-AgNPs as a new interface for electro-oxidation and determination of CA 125 in human plasma samples. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the direct determination of CA 125 based on P(CTAB-CS)-AgNPs films.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3), N‑hydroxysuccinimid (NHS), 1‑ethyl‑3‑(3‑dimethylaminopropyl)‑carbodiimide (EDC) and chitosan, citric acid, HNO3, NaOH, and CTAB were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium chloride, potassium chloride and potassium di-hydrogen phosphate (the component of phosphate buffer saline solution buffer), sodium hydroxide (Merck, Germany), were from analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Dopamine was obtained from Acros Organics, Germany. All solutions were prepared with deionized water (18.2 MΩ·cm, Zolalan Co., Iran). Human plasma samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran). Human CA 125 ELISA kit (CanAg/CA125 EIA) including CA 125 antigen, antibody (anti CA 125) standard, standard buffer, and dilute solutions were obtained from CanAg Diagnostic AB Technology Co. (Gothenburg, Sweden).

Electrochemical measurements were recorded using a computer-controlled electrochemical system comprising of AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands) using NOVA 1.11 software. The conventional three-electrode electrochemical cell was set using glassy carbon electrode (GCE), Ag/AgCl and a platinum wire as the working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively. The surface morphology of the modified electrodes and the nanoparticles was evaluated with a FESEM (field emission scanning electron microscopy) analysis which was conducted on a TESCAN system of FEG-SEM MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic). The elemental analysis of the electrode surface was recorded by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic) model.

Prior to modification, the GCE was polished to a mirror luster on a 0.1 μm alumina slurry on a microcloth polishing pad, followed by sonication in an ethanol/water bath for 5 min and electrochemically cleaned by cycling in PBS 1× for 10 scans in the potential range of −0.2 to 1.0 V (scan rate 0.1 V/s), until reproducible voltammograms were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of Ag NPs in the presence of CTAB
ce:section.ce:para: As was shown in Fig. 1A, GCE was chosen as the basal electrode, and the pretreated electrode was put in Ag NO3 solution containing 5 mM CTAB. The electrodes were treated by cyclic voltammetry in the potential range from −1.2 to +1.2 V for 20 cycles until stable voltammetric responses were obtained. After the electropolymerization, the modified electrodes were rinsed fully with distilled water and dried at room temperature.

Fig. 1A indicating that oxidation peak current decreased and disappeared sharply around potential 1.5 V, due to the oxidation of Br− to Br2. Based on the reducing reactivity of the active groups in the reduction processes, peak c indicates the reduction peak of Br2, and the reduction product was Br−. Because the concentration of Br2 was low in solution, the reduction peak of Br2 was low. The concentration of CTAB kept almost unchanged, so peak currents were slightly lower. With the number of scanning cycle increased, peak current decreased constantly, indicating polymer film formed gradually on the GCE surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of Ag NPs in the presence of CS
ce:section.ce:para: For electrodeposition of Ag NPs in the presence of chitosan, CV technique was used which the cycle number and scan rate were same if Section 2.3.1. As can be seen, similar results were obtained in this step of electrode modification (Fig. 1B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of Ag NPs in the presence of CTAB and CS
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous sub-section, for electrodeposition of Ag NPs in the presence of CTAB and CS, a mixes of AgNO3, HNO3, CS, and CTAB was prepared. Then, CVs of the solution were recorded in the 20 cycle in the scan rate of 50 mV/s. Fig. 1C shows the CVs of in-situ electrodeposition of Ag NPs in the presence of P(CTAB and CS) at the surface of GCE obtained by scanning potential over the range of −1.2 V to +1.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at 50 mV/s for 20 cycles. One well defined oxidation peak at +1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and in the reverse scan two reduction peak at 0.8 and +0.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), were observed in the CVs of electropolymerization process in HNO3 solution. The decrease of the oxidation and the increase of the reduction current densities with increasing cycle number of the potential scans were noticeable indicating the successful electropolymerization of the P(CTAB and CS) and Ag NPs on the electrode. At the end of the polymerization process, it was clear that a yellow-black polymer film was observable at GCE surface. This process arose from the successive electrochemical intercalation of the CTAB on the strongly hydrophobic graphite surface through electro-oxidation. Such behavior has been reported in previous studies in the literature [38,49]. During the electropolymerization process, the negatively charged CS was easily doped into poly (CTAB) structure through electrostatic interactions with cationic ammonium head group of surfactant. Also, Ag NPs were deposited on the surface of this nanostructure polymer.

In summary, the combination of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs on electrode surface improved the electron transfer rate and contributed to the enhancement in effective surface area of the modified electrode providing an easier way to immobilize HRP-labeled CA 125 antibody on the surface of electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of monoclonal antibody on the surface of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs and preparation of immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of immunosensor, 10 μL of 1 U/mL of HRP Labeled anti CA 125 was dropped on the surface of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE electrode and incubated in 4 °C for 5 h. Based on electrostatic interactions between the amine groups of antibody (HRP labeled anti CA 125) and the Ag NSs, the anti-CA 125 was immobilized on the surface of electrode. Following anti-CA 125 incubation, the electrodes were thoroughly rinsed with wash buffer (0.1% Tween-PBS (pH 8.5)) to remove the unbound anti-CA 125 protein. It is important to point out that, for activation of the COOH groups of antibodies, EDC/NHS chemistry was used where EDC (0.2 M) worked as a coupling agent and NHS (0.05 M) as an activator. A solution mixture of anti-CA 125, EDC and NHS in 2:1:1 ratio was made and kept at room temperature (25 °C) for 30 min. Then BSA solution (3%) was added to the modified surface and left for 1 h to block the free sites of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/Anti-CA 125/GCE surfaces to minimize the non-specific binding. BSA (0.1 M) was used for blocking the non-specific active sites. The immunosensor were directly used to electrochemical detection of CA 125. For CA 125 sensing, 10 μL of CA 125 antigen were dropped onto the sensor surface and left for 1 h. Thereafter, it was washed with PBS to remove any unbound antibody molecules. The fabricated P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA/CA 125/GCE immunosensor was washed with PBS and stored at 4 °C until further use. Prior to electrochemical measurements, the immunosensor were washed thoroughly with wash buffer.

Surface morphology of the bare and modified PGE surfaces was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 2 A–E represents the scanning electron micrographs of bare GCE (A), P (CTAB)-Ag NPs-GCE (B), P (CS)-Ag NPs-GCE (C), P (CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE (D), P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-Ab-GCE, and P (CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-Ab-BSA-CA 125-GCE respectively. Bare GCE has a rough surface due to irregular graphite sheets in its framework. Fig. 2B clearly shows that the morphology of the GCE changed after the electrodeposition of CTAB-Ag NPs by featuring a three-dimensional porous network structure. Similar results were obtained for P (CS)-Ag NPs-GCE (C). For the P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs composite, a totally different morphology was obtained indicating the formation of a nanocomposite film. Incorporation of CS within the poly (CTAB)-Ag NPs film during the electropolymerization involved polymer film growth on GCE. CS were embedded in the polymer film providing a more porous structure which results in increased surface area and improved electrical conductivity.

The coated layer provides a high surface area, which leads to enhance the antibody loading. A different morphology was developed over the electrode surface in the presence of antibody. The increased surface roughness implies antibody probe binding to the polymer.
Finally after immobilization of Ab and CA 125 antigen, morphology of the electrode were changed which confirmed successfully preparation of immunocomplex between antibody and antigen.
All of these results were confirmed by EDS analysis (Fig. 3 ).

The electrode modification steps were evaluated by CV technique in 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− containing KCl which has been shown in Fig. 4 . The characteristic peak of bare GCE was markedly increased after its modification with P(CTAB)-Ag NPs, P(CS)-Ag NPs, P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs. Interestingly, the data obtained results clearly show that the combination of CTAB and CS definitely alter the nature of CV oxidation peak current (low oxidation potential and high peak current). That might be related to the excellent property of P(CTAB)-Ag NPs, P(CS)-Ag NPs, P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs, such as high specific surface area and electrical conductivity. As can be obtained from CV curves, the oxidation peak current of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs increased in accordance with the increase of the effective surface area after modifying mixture. Presence of a highly conductive Ag NPs layer on the P(CTAB-CS) surface with a large geometric area shows the signal amplification for the redox probe, due to the improvement of the electron transfer process. As it is obvious, changes in the current responses are completely distinguishable from CV, which highlights the CV capability in tracing small changes on this conductive platform in the presence of other reagents compare to the represented voltammograms in the same condition. Therefore, to make a more stable and promising immunosensing platform further modification requires creating more binding sites to enhance sensitivity. Metal NPs provides a high surface area and which improve their dispersivity, stability and sensitivity. Thus, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were found more suitable for immunosensor application. Subsequently, P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs provide free amine group for interaction with a carboxylic group of Fc region of antibody via a covalent bond. Thus, it provides a favorable condition for immobilization of the antibody against antigen. Upon immobilization of HRP labeled-anti-CA 125, a decline in peak current was observed. In fact, the antibodies act as an insulter and reduce the electron transfer rate. After blocking the nonspecific binding sites by BSA, the peak current further decreased. This could be attributed to the blocking effect of BSA on the electron transfer process. Following dropping of antigen on the surface of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/Ab/BSA, the peak current was decreased again, because of the creation of the immunocomplex reaction antigen-antibody.

The electroactive surface area (A) values, that are different from the geometric one of different electrodes, are measured by peak current versus scan rate (Fig. 5A–D). The Values of different electrodes are calculated by CVs using the equation Γ =
Q nFA
. In the above equation, Γ is the surface coverage of the electrode reaction substance, Q is the charge involved in the reaction, which is calculated from the reduction peak area of the voltammogram by integrating the reduction current against time (Q = ∫idt). n is the number of electron transferred reaction, F is Faraday constant, and A is the electrode surface area. The A value of P(CTAB)-Ag NPs and P(CS)-Ag NPs, is 4.47 × 10−6 cm2 s−1 and 4.47 × 10−6 cm2 s−1, respectively, while P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs has a high active surface area with a value of 1.26 × 10−6 cm2 s−1, indicating that: a) CS has a binding to CTAB in the polymerization procedure and b) Ag NPs was successfully deposited. Also, P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs increases the active surface area (6.12 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), and thus improves the electron transfer. Therefore, this nanocomposite (P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs) alters the surface volume ratio and enhances the electro-catalytic activity. Finally, the diffusion layer thickness of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs leads to higher peak currents due to an increase in the particle size and active surface area with an A value of 7.35 × 10−6 cm2 s−1. Besides, as the microscopic area increases due to the presence of CTAB, CS, and Ag NPs, the available electroactive sites increase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical characteristic of the CA 125 immunosensor

ce:section.ce:para:
According to the obtained results, it was possible to apply this technique to the quantitative analysis of CA 125 in human real samples using DPV and SWV techniques (Figs. 8 and 9
, respectively). As mentioned previously, the 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− containing KCl was selected as the supporting electrolyte for the quantification of CA 125. For this purpose, 10 μL of unprocessed human plasma samples were mixed with different concentrations of CA 125 and incubated on the surface of electrode. The, DPVs and SWVs were recorded in 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− containing KCl to reach a total volume of 5 mL. Using DPV and SWV techniques, the resulting sample solutions were measured using the prepared immunosensor.

To check the inter-electrode reproducibility of the modified electrode, four modified electrodes were tested simultaneously by recording DPVs in buffer solution, containing 15 U/mL of CA 125. The relative standard deviation was 4.33%. Therefore, P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA imparts a suitable stability onto measurements of CA 125 by DPV and SWV techniques.

The stability of the developed method was investigated as a key factor affecting the analytical performance of the present immunosensor. In order to confirm the stability of the prepared immunosensor, we utilized the DPV technique to investigate voltammetric behavior. The voltammetric behavior of the proposed CA 125 immunosensor in the solution containing 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− is very stable and reduction in peak current was observed after 24 h repetitive cycles (Fig. 10 A). It can be proposed that the long term stability is related to the high chemical and mechanical stability of the P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE nanocomposites and covalent attachment of antibody functionalized AgNPs on the surface of the modified electrode; therefore, this nanocomposite indicates promising properties as a useful platform for immobilization of antibody and preparation of immunosensor. For confirmation, cyclic stability of P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs were investigated by CV techniques. As can be seen, no change was observed on the redox peak currents/potential which confirms high stability of the organic-inorganic platform in aqueous solution (Fig. 10B).

Control experiments were performed in the presence of some coexistent proteins as interferences to evaluate the immunosensor selectivity. For this purpose, DPV and SWV experiments were carried out to check the cross-reactivity of the CA 125 (15 U/mL) with possible coexisting and interfering compounds, such as BSA, CA 15-3, PSA, glucose, and uric acid. The prepared immunosensor (P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA) was incubated with interfering compounds in the supporting electrolyte. The peak current produced by the P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA is almost similar (Fig. 11 ). The successive addition of higher concentrations of interfering compounds did not make any appreciable increment in peak current, shows that the proposed sensor exhibits remarkable selectivity towards CA 125. This can be attributed to the suitable specificity of the P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs/GCE/anti-CA 125/BSA immunosensor to detection of CA 125.

The recovery assessment of the CA-125 immunosensor was carried out by spiking serum samples with diverse concentrations of CA-125 (Table 2 ). The recovery of the electrode was calculated as percentage using the following equation:
R % =
spiked sample result – unspiked sample result
× 100 %
known spike added
con c centration
Excellent recoveries varying from 99.1% to 109.33% were obtained, which illustrates that the established method are quite desirable and qualified for practical sample testing.

In this work, an innovative immunoassay for quantitation of CA 125 based on signal amplification strategy was proposed. In this work, Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) labeled CA 125-antibody (anti-CA 125) was immobilized onto a green and biocompatible nanocomposite containing poly cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (P(CTAB)) as conductive matrix, chitosan (CS) as biocompatible agent and sliver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) as signal amplification element. Therefore, a novel nanocomposite film based P (CTAB-CS) and Ag NPs was exploited to develop a highly sensitive immunosensor for detection of CA 125 protein. Importantly, Ag NPs prepared by electrodeposition method which lead to compact morphology. Fully electrochemical methodology was used to prepare a new transducer on a glassy carbon surface which provided a high surface area to immobilize a high amount of the anti-CA 125. Under optimized condition the calibration curve for CA 125 concentration by SWV and DPV was linear in 00.01–400 U/mL with lower limit of quantification of 0.001 U/mL. The method was successfully applied assay of the CA 125 in unprocessed human plasma samples. Above all, the prepared aptasensor can be an extremely promising candidate applicable to and specific and convenient for, a wide range of electrochemical immunosensing applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences and Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University.
